74798.subject: 💀 OpenAI kills prompting
74798.body:
----------
######                  [Collaborate with us](https://6hhfxazcbpc.typeform.com/to/uXIl0IFH?utm_source=atlas&utm_medium=newsletter&utm_campaign=general_2026_05_11&utm_content=banner_post)


--------------------
View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/8776fa6e-e2d0-4aec-9648-1bed6de3a659/GenAI_Daily_Banner.jpg?t=1778687445)
Caption: 


----------

----------
**Welcome back!** The wall between the AI labs and the US government is getting thin. OpenAI hired the man who helped write Trump's AI plan, while every major lab now hands its models to Washington for testing before they ship. On the product side, OpenAI and Anthropic shipped rival upgrades to their coding agents within an hour of each other last night. Busy day, so let's get into it.



**In today's Generative AI Newsletter:**

* **Codex:** How do you teach an AI agent a task without writing a single prompt?

* **Washington:** What does it mean that every AI lab now lets the government test its models before launch?

* **OpenAI:** Why did OpenAI put Trump's AI-plan author on payroll?

* **Claude Code:** What happens when your coding session turns into a page your whole team can watch?


----------

----------
## [OpenAI says stop prompting](https://x.com/openaidevs/status/2067681320281723113)

View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/a54ddd14-197a-4070-8a33-98cb0dbac11c/Beehiiv_04_-_Jun_19.png?t=1781859610)
Caption: 

OpenAI added a feature to Codex that retires the prompt for repetitive work. It's called Record and Replay. 

You do a boring task once on your Mac, like filing an expense report, while Codex watches over your shoulder. 

It turns that demo into a skill it can rerun later with new dates or files, and you can open the skill and edit it.

This is a different way to teach an agent. 

Instead of describing a chore in a paragraph and hoping the model gets it, you show it once and it keeps the recipe. For anything that's easier to demonstrate than explain, that's the way to do it.

The fine print keeps it from being a today thing for most people. It runs only on macOS, you need OpenAI's Computer Use turned on, and it isn't in the EU yet. 

The direction is the real headline though. The prompt was always a workaround for not being able to show the computer what you mean.


----------



----------
_Special highlight from our network_

## [By Sunday night, you'll have Claude running your desktop](https://link.genai.works/wxfc)

View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/90270db5-f312-4d53-8592-80615383aeb8/image.png?t=1781858802)
Follow image link: (https://link.genai.works/wxfc)
Caption: 

Anthropic ships new Claude capabilities almost every week. Skills, Conne

74797.subject: How to Pre-Mortem Any Decision With AI: The Future Failure Audit Skill for Claude 🔮
74797.body:
View this post on the web at https://linas.substack.com/p/ai-pre-mortem-claude-skill

👋 Hey, Linas here! Every day, I break down 3 stories shaping the future of FinTech & Artificial Intelligence - plus the money movements and trends worth tracking. First time here? 395k+ FinTech and AI leaders get this daily. Join them:
Most expensive mistakes don’t start as obviously bad ideas. They start as plausible plans that nobody stress-tested while there was still time to change them.
The launch that looked ready. The hire who interviewed perfectly. The round that should have closed. The investment memo that read well right up until it didn’t.
A pre-mortem fixes this.
Instead of asking “what could go wrong?”, it assumes the plan has already failed and works backward to explain exactly how it died, while you can still do something about it.
The technique rests on a documented cognitive effect called prospective hindsight: research by Deborah Mitchell, Jay Russo, and Nancy Pennington found that imagining a future event has already happened makes people roughly 30% better at identifying the reasons it would (Mitchell et al., 1989; Klein, HBR 2007). Nobel laureate in economics Daniel Kahneman later endorsed the pre-mortem as one of the few practical defenses against overconfidence, groupthink, and the planning fallacy.
The problem is that almost nobody runs one. Pre-mortems are awkward to organize, easy to skip, and quietly threatening to a team that has already fallen in love with its plan. The single highest-return decision tool in behavioral science thus sits unused while preventable failures stack up.
This guide therefore turns the pre-mortem into something you can run in 60 seconds, on any decision, indefinitely: a packaged Future Failure Audit skill for Claude. Upload it once, and Claude becomes a structured adversary on demand. It reconstructs your plan, generates specific failure modes, scores them by likelihood and danger, surfaces the hidden assumption everyone treated as settled, and hands you a revised plan with owners, early-warning metrics, and kill criteria.
Here’s what’s inside:
The exact, complete SKILL.md file you can upload to Claude today.
The decision science behind why pre-mortems work, in language you can use to win over a skeptical team.
Five worked playbooks for product launches, fundraises, hires, strategy decisions, and investments.
A real example output showing a launch getting torn apart and rebuilt into something that survives.
The “weak input vs. strong input” rule that separates a useless pre-mortem from one that changes a decision.
How it plugs into the 12-skill AI startup operating system [ https://substack.com/redirect/fbeece6e-043e-4a89-878e-c122556eb174?j=eyJ1IjoiMXN4Zm00In0.wehL2Vke0PUDz1PHD6ZKtcy1ufIoeqpblecCEEWPpqI ] as the missing 13th skill - the one that stress-tests every plan the other twelve produce before you execute.
Step-by-step install instructions for both Claude Code [ https://substack.com/r

CMC Market Pulse:
The Essential Weekly Crypto Briefing

Welcome to this week’s CMC Market Pulse newsletter!

CMC Market Pulse delivers actionable market insights, giving you
all the key narratives and developments you need in under 5
minutes.

Let's dive into this week.

Market Overview 📈
-----------------

Bitcoin (BTC) continues to weaken this week, falling
0.99%,
despite a strong start to the week. Ethereum (ETH) interestingly
climbed higher, rising
1.79%
in the same week. Total crypto market cap, however, stayed flat
for the week, stalling at
$2.15T.

Weekly liquidations cooled down from the week prior, resetting
to usual levels of $3-400M in position liquidated daily. While
short liquidations jumped at the start of the week, long
liquidations caught up leading into the end of the week. Funding
rates show no clear signal, as the market remains divided, with
several rising over the week, and others falling instead.

* Michael Saylor’s Strategy announces the purchase of 1,587 BTC
for $100M, bringing their total holdings to 846,842.
* Binance is set to lose permission to operate in the EU under
the new MiCA laws, after its application is set to be rejected by
the Greek market regulator.
* Blackrock launches their Bitcoin Premium Income ETF (BITA),
which generates yield on Bitcoin holdings via options premium.
An incredible week for traditional equities this week as
markets ride the positive se
